clean_story:
Plastic’s silent digesters. Fungi with an appetite.

The name plastic-eating mushrooms refers to fungi that break down polyesters—like a chef slicing spaghetti, but at a molecular level.

Most plastics resist decay for centuries. These mushrooms do the opposite: their root-like mycelium secretes enzymes that dismantle plastic chains into simpler compounds, turning waste into food. One species, Aspergillus tubingensis, was found growing on a dump in Pakistan in 2017. In weeks, it decomposed polyester polyurethane, a plastic used in shoes and refrigerators, by 90 percent when buried in soil. Another, Pestalotiopsis microspora, thrives in oxygen-free landfills, breaking down polyethylene—the same material in grocery bags—without sunlight or air.

The process isn’t fast. A single mushroom colony might take months to consume a credit-card-sized piece. But unlike recycling, which often downgrades plastic, fungi turn it into biomass or carbon dioxide, leaving no microplastics behind. Scientists now engineer "myco-remediation" sites, where contaminated soil is inoculated with these strains. In Ecuador, a startup uses Pleurotus ostreatus (oyster mushrooms) to clean oil-spilled farms; the same species also degrades polystyrene foam.

Three related subjects are fungal communication networks, biodegradable packaging alternatives, and how enzymes unlock recycling.
